critique.summary: The article discusses the reconstruction of VisiCalc, the first spreadsheet software created in 1979, by implementing a minimal clone in C. It explains the core concepts of spreadsheets such as cells, formulas, and recalculation, and demonstrates how to build a simple text-based user interface similar to the original VisiCalc.
mainPoints: VisiCalc was the first spreadsheet software, created by Dan Bricklin and Bob Frankston in 1979, and was a killer app for the Apple II.
The article reconstructs a minimal VisiCalc clone using C, focusing on the data model, formula evaluation, and a simple terminal UI.
Formulas in VisiCalc start with a '+' instead of '=', and the implementation supports basic arithmetic, cell references, and some functions like SUM and ABS.
Recalculation is done by repeatedly evaluating all formulas until no changes occur, mimicking VisiCalc's manual recalculation approach.
A modal text-based user interface is built using the ncurses library, replicating VisiCalc's screen layout and interaction style.
The article acknowledges missing features such as file I/O, advanced commands, and larger grids but covers the essential spreadsheet mechanics.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the history and significance of VisiCalc, including its creators, release year, and impact on personal computing. The technical details about the spreadsheet data model, formula parsing, and recalculation methods are consistent with known implementations of early spreadsheet software. The description of the user interface and modal input aligns with documented behavior of VisiCalc. The code examples and explanations are plausible and reflect a faithful minimal re-implementation. The article does not make unsupported factual claims and provides links to source code for verification.
considerations: The article does not discuss the limitations of the minimal implementation in terms of performance or scalability compared to modern spreadsheets.
There is no mention of how the reconstructed VisiCalc handles circular references or error propagation beyond using NAN.
The user interface is limited to terminal environments and does not cover graphical interfaces or modern usability improvements.
The article omits discussion of file saving/loading, which is critical for practical spreadsheet use.
Security considerations, such as injection risks in formula parsing, are not addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the complexity and implementation details of spreadsheet software, particularly VisiCalc and its modern reconstructions. Contributors share personal projects, technical insights on dependency graphs and parsers, and historical context about early spreadsheet adoption and programming challenges.

comments.author: fouronnes3
comments.text:
Very cool article!

I also implemented a spreadsheet last year [0] in pure TypeScript, with the fun twist that formulas also update backwards. While the backwards root finding algorithm was challenging, I also found it incredibly humbling to discover how much complexity there is in the UX of the simple spreadsheet interface. Handling selection states, reactive updates, detecting cycles of dependency and gracefully recovering from them is a massive state machine programming challenge! Very fun project with a lot of depth!

I myself didn't hand roll my own parser but used Ohm-js [1] which I highly recommend if you want to parse a custom language in Javascript or TypeScript.

> One way of doing this is to keep track of all dependencies between the cells and trigger updates when necessary. Maintaining a dependency graph would give us the most efficient updates, but it’s often an overkill for a spreadsheet.

On that subject, figuring out the efficient way to do it is also a large engineering challenge, and is definitely not overkill but absolutely required for a modern spreadsheet implementation. There is a good description of how Excel does it in this famous paper "Build systems a la carte" paper, which interestingly takes on a spreadsheet as a build system [2].

[0] https://victorpoughon.github.io/bidicalc/

[1] https://ohmjs.org/

[2] https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/wp-content/uploads/...
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed technical insights and references credible sources, making it highly accurate and constructive.

comments.author: airstrike
comments.text:
> Maintaining a dependency graph would give us the most efficient updates, but it’s often an overkill for a spreadsheet.

It's not overkill at all. In fact, it's absolutely necessary for all but the simplest toy examples.
comments.analysis: This comment corrects a misconception and aligns with established spreadsheet engineering principles, adding valuable clarification.

comments.author: staplung
comments.analysis: This comment identifies a specific inconsistency between the article and code, highlighting the importance of accurate documentation.

comments.author: afandian
comments.analysis: Raises a valid technical concern about hardware constraints relevant to historical spreadsheet implementations.

comments.author: manoDev
comments.text: VisiCalc has, undoubtedly, the highest impact-to-complexity ratio in the history of software so far.
comments.analysis: A subjective but widely accepted assessment of VisiCalc's significance in software development history.

comments.author: ivanpribec
comments.text: Reminds me of spreadsheet-fortran (https://github.com/lwsinclair/spreadsheet-fortran), a project creating a VisiCalc lookalike in FORTRAN 66, which even runs on a PDP-11.
comments.analysis: Provides relevant historical context and a link to a related project, enhancing the discussion.

comments.author: rnxrx
comments.text: This a great article - both interesting and potentially really useful to folks teaching- or learning- programming.
comments.analysis: Expresses positive sentiment about the article's educational value but adds little technical content.

comments.author: mlhpdx
comments.text:
This was one of the projects students did when I helped teach APCS to high schoolers as a TEALS volunteer (FracCalc).

Some of the implementations went way overboard and it was so much fun to watch and to play a part.

Even as a “seasoned” developer I learned some tidbits talking through the ways to do (and not do) certain parts. When to store input raw vs processed, etc.
comments.analysis: Provides a practical perspective on educational use of spreadsheet projects, supporting the article's relevance.

comments.author: bonsai_spool
comments.text: Are there good command-line interfaces for spreadsheets? I don't do anything super financially-important and I'd prefer to stay in the terminal for quick editing of things, especially if I can have Vi keybindings.
comments.analysis: Poses a relevant question that could guide further discussion or resource sharing.

comments.author: khazhoux
comments.text: I’m genuinely worried that we’re the last generation who will study and appreciate this craft.  Because now a kid learning to program will just say “Write me a terminal spreadsheet app in plain C.”
comments.analysis: Expresses a nostalgic concern about programming education but is speculative.

comments.author: tracker1
comments.text:
Kinda cool to see... TBH, I'd be more inclined to reach for Rust and Ratatui myslf over C + ncurses. I know this would likely be a much larger executable though.

With MS Edit resurrected similarly, I wonder how hard it would be to get a flushed out text based spreadsheet closer in function to MS Excel or Lotus 123 versions for DOS, but cross platform. Maybe even able to load/save a few different formats from CSV/TSV to XLSX (without OLE/COM embeds).
comments.analysis: Offers a modern perspective on technology choices and potential future projects.

comments.author: airstrike
comments.analysis: Provides a resource that complements the discussion but lacks detailed commentary.

comments.author: 4leafclover
comments.analysis: Offers a technical correction consistent with other comments about parser struct definition.

comments.author: breadsniffer
comments.text: Anyone know what kind of departments/parts of business were the first adopters of visicalc?
comments.analysis: Poses a historical question that could enrich the discussion but does not provide information.

comments.author: ksr
comments.text: Dan Bricklin wrote a JavaScript spreadsheet engine around 2008 https://github.com/DanBricklin/socialcalc. I extended it and adapted it to Drupal CMS around that time https://www.drupal.org/project/sheetnode.
comments.analysis: Adds historical and technical context relevant to spreadsheet software evolution.
